hook: You already know how to integrate functions along intervals and over regions — but the real world is full of quantities like wind velocity, electric fields, and fluid flow that live on curves and surfaces in three-dimensional space. Standard integrals can't capture how a force pushes an object along a winding path, or how much fluid escapes through a curved surface. This unit hands you the tools to do exactly that, culminating in three of the most beautiful theorems in all of mathematics.
concept: Vector calculus extends integration and differentiation to vector fields — quantities that have both magnitude and direction at every point in space — unifying everything through the single powerful idea of a 'generalized fundamental theorem': the integral of a derivative over a region equals a simpler integral over its boundary, expressed formally as $\int_\Omega d\omega = \int_{\partial\Omega} \omega$.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to visualize and describe functions like $\mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \langle P, Q, R \rangle$ that assign a vector to every point in space, forming the foundation for everything that follows.
Master integrating both scalar functions and vector fields along curves in space, enabling you to compute quantities like work done by a force along an arbitrary path.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to recognize conservative vector fields and use their potential functions to evaluate line integrals instantly, regardless of the path taken.
Apply Green's Theorem to convert difficult line integrals around closed curves in the plane into more tractable double integrals over the enclosed region, and vice versa.

You will learn to compute and interpret curl ($\nabla \times \mathbf{F}$) and divergence ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}$), the two key differential operators that measure rotation and expansion within a vector field.
Master parametrizing surfaces in three dimensions and integrating scalar functions and vector fields over them, laying the groundwork for the great theorems ahead.

You will apply Stokes' Theorem to relate the surface integral of curl over a surface to the line integral around its boundary, generalizing both Green's Theorem and the Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals.
Apply the Divergence Theorem to convert flux integrals over closed surfaces into triple integrals over the enclosed volume, completing the grand unifying picture of vector calculus.

You will discover how differential forms provide the elegant, unified language — $\int_\Omega d\omega = \int_{\partial\Omega} \omega$ — that reveals Green's, Stokes', and the Divergence Theorem as special cases of a single profound idea.
